---
title: Experience
---

```{=html}
<div class="page-shell page-shell-inner">
  <section class="section reveal">
    <div class="section-heading">
      <p class="eyebrow">Experience</p>
      <h2>Career path</h2>
    </div>
    <div class="timeline">
      <article class="timeline-item">
        <div class="timeline-marker"></div>
        <div class="timeline-content">
          <p class="timeline-period">2026-present</p>
          <h3>Senior Director, Biostatistics</h3>
          <p class="timeline-org">Datavant (formerly Aetion)</p>
          <p>
            I lead the biostatistics and statistical programming functions within Datavant's
            Science organization. My work focuses on building best practices for regulatory-grade
            real-world evidence studies, developing internal tools like R packages and Shiny
            applications, and partnering with product teams on future opportunities, including
            thoughtful uses of AI. It is a role that combines scientific quality, team leadership,
            and operational execution.
          </p>
        </div>
      </article>
      <article class="timeline-item">
        <div class="timeline-marker"></div>
        <div class="timeline-content">
          <p class="timeline-period">September 2023-present</p>
          <h3>Lead Real World Evidence Biostatistician, Medical Affairs</h3>
          <p class="timeline-org">Tempus AI</p>
          <p>
            At Tempus AI, I collaborated with external KOLs on public-facing, peer-reviewed
            genomic oncology research using real-world clinical and molecular data. I acted as a
            statistical lead for Medical Affairs by reviewing study proposals, developing
            statistical analysis plans, refining best practices for cohort extraction and
            programming, and mentoring junior biostatisticians.
          </p>
          <p>
            I also led biostatistical work within Clinical Development related to treatment
            response monitoring and minimal residual disease in advanced cancers, and developed AI
            tools, internal agents, and reusable packages to improve efficiency and standardize
            workflows.
          </p>
        </div>
      </article>
      <article class="timeline-item">
        <div class="timeline-marker"></div>
        <div class="timeline-content">
          <p class="timeline-period">June 2022-August 2023</p>
          <h3>Biostatistician, Health Data Analysis, Computational Biology</h3>
          <p class="timeline-org">23andMe Therapeutics</p>
          <p>
            At 23andMe, I worked as a biostatistical partner on cross-functional teams supporting
            preclinical therapeutic pipelines through observational study design, applied analyses,
            and methodologic work in bias correction, disease clustering, and causal inference.
          </p>
          <p>
            I also supported ongoing oncology clinical trial programs through statistical input on
            analyses and sample size calculations while overseeing work performed by external CROs.
          </p>
        </div>
      </article>
      <article class="timeline-item">
        <div class="timeline-marker"></div>
        <div class="timeline-content">
          <p class="timeline-period">September 2021-June 2022</p>
          <h3>Assistant Professor, Biostatistics</h3>
          <p class="timeline-org">University of Kentucky, College of Public Health</p>
          <p>
            I collaborated as a co-investigator on the design, analysis, interpretation, and
            reporting of observational and randomized biomedical studies, along with externally
            funded research proposals. I also supported the NIH-funded CCTS BERD core, consulting
            on study design, sample selection, and statistical analysis for projects that often
            relied on claims and electronic health record data.
          </p>
          <p>
            This role also included manuscript peer review, graduate student oversight, and broad
            collaboration across translational research teams.
          </p>
        </div>
      </article>
      <article class="timeline-item">
        <div class="timeline-marker"></div>
        <div class="timeline-content">
          <p class="timeline-period">January 2021-June 2022</p>
          <h3>Lead Biostatistician / Lead Biomedical Data Scientist</h3>
          <p class="timeline-org">
            University of Kentucky, Biostatistics Consulting and Interdisciplinary Research
            Collaboration Lab (CIRCL)
          </p>
          <p>
            I was a founding member of CIRCL and helped develop best practices for project
            management, statistical consulting, programming, visualization, reporting, and results
            communication. I also managed and mentored staff biostatisticians and biomedical data
            scientists while serving as a statistical and investigational lead for multiple
            cross-functional research groups.
          </p>
          <p>
            A major part of the role was leading and reviewing statistical analysis plans for
            external projects and collaborative research efforts.
          </p>
        </div>
      </article>
      <article class="timeline-item">
        <div class="timeline-marker"></div>
        <div class="timeline-content">
          <p class="timeline-period">May 2015-August 2021</p>
          <h3>Biostatistician</h3>
          <p class="timeline-org">University of Kentucky, College of Medicine</p>
          <p>
            I collaborated independently with departments across the College of Medicine on study
            design, sample selection, statistical analysis, interpretation, and dissemination for
            biomedical research. The work spanned surgical oncology, urology, plastic surgery,
            cardiothoracic surgery, cardiology, pediatrics, emergency medicine, radiology, and
            more.
          </p>
        </div>
      </article>
      <article class="timeline-item">
        <div class="timeline-marker"></div>
        <div class="timeline-content">
          <p class="timeline-period">Training</p>
          <h3>PhD in Biostatistics</h3>
          <p class="timeline-org">University of Kentucky</p>
          <p>
            My doctoral training centered on dementia, genetics, observational analysis, and
            translational health science. It shaped the statistical rigor that still anchors how I
            approach evidence generation today.
          </p>
        </div>
      </article>
    </div>
  </section>

  <section class="section reveal">
    <div class="section-heading">
      <p class="eyebrow">Methods and Platforms</p>
      <h2>Technical range</h2>
    </div>
    <div class="card-grid">
      <article class="info-card">
        <h3>Statistical Computing</h3>
        <p>
          Extensive experience in R, RStudio/Posit, R Markdown, ggplot2, R Shiny, GitHub/version
          control, SAS, LaTeX, and cluster computing environments including Slurm.
        </p>
      </article>
      <article class="info-card">
        <h3>Real-World and Clinical Methods</h3>
        <p>
          Applied work in observational study design, causal inference, time-to-event analysis,
          competing risks, mixed models, generalized estimating equations, and clinical research
          support.
        </p>
      </article>
      <article class="info-card">
        <h3>Modern Analytic Workflows</h3>
        <p>
          Experience building analytic pipelines, statistical packages, visualization tools, and
          reusable reporting workflows that help teams move from ad hoc analyses to more durable
          systems.
        </p>
      </article>
      <article class="info-card">
        <h3>Genomics and Omics Data</h3>
        <p>
          Familiar with genomic and molecular analysis workflows including RNA-seq, NanoString,
          GWAS, gene-based inference, eQTL analyses, and related translational applications.
        </p>
      </article>
    </div>
  </section>
</div>
```
